question: According to Lecture 2, which Nobel Prize-winning biologist described the idea that 'you're nothing but a pack of neurons' as 'The Astonishing Hypothesis'?
In Lecture 2, what is the name of the disorder, discovered by Paul Broca in 1861, in which a patient with brain damage could only say one word, 'tan'?
In Lecture 2, Bloom states that approximately what percentage of the volume of the human brain is cortex?

According to Freud's theory of psychosexual stages in Lecture 3, which mythical story involving a king who killed his father and married his mother is the basis for the complex occurring during the phallic stage?
In Lecture 3, which philosopher introduced the term 'falsifiability' as the criterion distinguishing science from non-science?
In the Norbert Schwarz demonstration described in Lecture 3, what happened to participants' liking of a loved one when asked to list ten positive features versus three?
In Lecture 4, what is the name of the baby in John Watson's famous stimulus generalization experiment who was conditioned to fear a rat?
In Lecture 4, what does Bloom say negative reinforcement consists of (distinguishing it from punishment)?
In Lecture 4, whose 'Review of Verbal Behavior' is described as the document that could be said to have destroyed behaviorism?
In Lecture 5, what term did Piaget use for the understanding that things continue to exist even when you no longer see them?

According to Lecture 6, what is the term for a makeshift communication system (strings of words borrowed from different languages put together haphazardly) that develops among people from different language backgrounds?
In Lecture 6, Bloom cites which linguist's book The Language Instinct as the source for his discussion of phonology, morphology, and syntax?
According to Lecture 6, Ferdinand de Saussure described the property by which any arbitrary idea can be connected to a sound or sign as what?
In Lecture 7, which researcher commented that if a normally developing child spoke like the chimpanzee Nim, his parents would rush him screaming to a neurologist?
In Lecture 7, what is the name for the claim that the structural properties of the language you know affect the way you think (linguistic relativity)?
According to Lecture 8, cognitive psychologist George Miller stated the standard storage capacity of short-term memory is what number?
In Lecture 8, which researcher's eyewitness studies showed that asking whether cars 'smash' versus 'hit' changed whether subjects remembered broken glass?
In Lecture 9, according to Robert Sternberg's theory of love, what are the three components that make up love?
In Lecture 9, what is the name of the effect in which our liking for a competent person increases when they make a blunder?
In Lecture 10, in Kahneman and Tversky's base-rate disease problem (1 in 1000 prevalence, 5% false positive), what is the approximate probability you have the disease if you test positive?
In Lecture 11, what was the weight of the iron rod that passed through Phineas Gage's head, and in what year did the accident occur?
What name is given to the genuine happiness smile that involves movement around the eyes?
According to Lecture 11, approximately what fraction of people can fake a Duchenne (genuine happiness) smile?
What did the biologist Haldane reply when asked whether he would lay down his life for his brother?
In Lecture 12, what was the name of the simple program that won Robert Axelrod's Prisoner's Dilemma tournament, and who developed it?
Which two psychologists studied the American South's 'culture of honor' using Michigan undergraduates bumped and insulted in a hallway?
In Lecture 13, the mnemonic 'OCEAN' captures the Big Five personality factors. What do the letters stand for?
What is the Flynn effect, as defined in Lecture 13?
According to the James Gleick data cited in Lecture 14, what is the average time per day the average American devotes to sex?
In Lecture 15, what name does Jonathan Haidt give to the phenomenon where people condemn something but struggle to explain why it's wrong?
In Stanley Milgram's package-passing study in Lecture 16, how many people in Omaha were given a package, and where did the target stockbroker live?
According to Lecture 16, who is identified as the most connected actor in the history of acting?
In Festinger's cognitive dissonance experiment in Lecture 16, which group rated the boring task as more fun: those paid one dollar or twenty dollars?
In Lecture 17, in Rosenthal and Ambady's 'thin slices' research, how many seconds of watching a professor do people need to predict their teaching evaluations?
What does Bloom say is the single most common dream, according to Lecture 17?
In Lecture 18, what is the term for the diminished interest or pleasure that is one of the two required first criteria for major depression in the DSM?
According to Lecture 18, for identical (monozygotic) twins, what is the approximate chance of having bipolar disorder if your twin has it, versus a fraternal twin?
In Lecture 18, Aaron Beck's 'negative cognitive triad' says depressed people hold a negative view of which three things?
In Lecture 19, what change in the number of dissociative identity disorder cases in the US does Bloom cite between 1930-1960 and the 1980s?
In the happiness research in Lecture 20, which nationality is named as the happiest, and which as the saddest, in the 42-country study?
How many students were enrolled in PSYC 110 when it was recorded for Open Yale Courses?
What is the name of the head teaching assistant for PSYC 110?
What minimum grade does a student need to pass PSYC 110?
What is the molecular formula of the neurotransmitter dopamine?
How many minutes long is the final exam for this course?
What is the location of Professor Paul Bloom's office at Yale?
In which year did Paul Bloom join the Yale faculty?
On what calendar date is the PSYC 110 final exam scheduled?
What is the assigned textbook reading for Lecture 12?
In which academic journal was Festinger's cognitive dissonance study first published?
